We've mentioned financial covenants several times in this module and I've covered them at a very high level. Covenant essentially means contract or deal. There are conditions that will be included in the loan agreement that you agree to fall at a lender's request. There are a few different types you might encounter. Affirmative loan covenants, financial loan covenants, and negative loan covenants. Typically require you to do something such as purchase adequate insurance, maintain the underlying assets or collateral, or deliver audit or financial statements. Financial loan covenants usually ask you to maintain something. You'll set benchmarks, metrics, or ratios that act as a vital sign of the business to measure profitability and cash flow performance, essentially the health, financial health of your business. Since the lenders aren't on site at the business, these indicators keep them informed on how the business is actually doing so they know whether it's on track or not. The negative loan covenants act like restrictions. These create boundaries that restrict you from doing certain things like paying out dividends, selling assets, or taking out loans against the business. You will find these covenants in every loan agreement you do in some form. Since the lender isn't there, they need to be able to monitor the business and manage it from a distance. So they'll ask you to report on these covenant items periodically. These covenants are usually negotiable to an extent and some of these covenants aren't always a requirement, meaning you can reject them as part of the agreement. Which is why you want to have options, because different lenders will allow you to negotiate. Now let's dive deeper into each covenant. An affirmative loan covenant requires the borrow to perform certain actions and meet specific benchmarks. Like all these covenants, it acts as an investor protection. If there's any kind of issue, then the covenant will call for some kind of remediation for the lender. Many covenants will outline some kind of grace period in case of a default where the borrower can fix the violation. However, if it isn't resolved, the loan then immediately defaults and the borrower must pay the full principal and any accrued interest. The requirements in the covenant might be as basic as keeping proper accounting, maintaining some kind of credit rating, or complying with regulations or laws, things that the borrower should already be striving for. The investor just wants some reassurance and contractual requirements in place to protect them in case that becomes an issue. Some other requirements might be requiring that the business maintain some adequate level of insurance. Some covenants will be very specific and others will be quite broad. So you'll want to read everything very carefully before you sign. Financial loan covenants require you to maintain certain things. A common one is liquidity. They'll ask that you calculate a liquidity ratio on a regular basis and they might require that ratio to be above a certain threshold. For the lender, having liquidity in their investment improves the likelihood that they'll get paid back from the loan. Another metric that they'll look at is debt to equity ratio, which compares liabilities to shareholder equity. It can be used to evaluate how much leverage a company is using. A higher debt to equity ratio usually indicates a company that is a higher risk to lenders, so they'll require you to have a certain ratio there as well. Another typical financial loan covenant is maintaining certain working capital minimums. They may also look at your credit balance, how much money you owe versus how much is owed to you. Lenders will use their preferred matrix to measure the business and confirm that it's running in a way they expect based on your agreement. Lastly, we have negative loan covenants. Unlike affirmative loan covenants where you're expecting to do something with negative covenants, you're expected to not do something. These restrict certain actions by the borrower unless agreed upon by the lender. A common covenant here is restrictions on distributions from the business that can be made to shareholders as dividends. They don't want you sucking all the money out of the business until they get paid, I guarantee you. Another could prevent management fees from being paid to relatives or related parties. For example, the lender might not want you paying your family from the business while they're still owed money. Another might be buying and selling the company's assets or even companies without their permission. If those assets are part of the security for the loan, then the covenant will be included to require you not to sell assets. Many of the restrictions these covenants outline attempt to limit the borrower's ability to compromise the loan repayment. Remember, the lenders are always at a distance. To continue doing their job, they need to know what's going on in the company so they can ensure the money is going to be paid back. And when borrowers go out of their covenants, the lender actually has to report them. Sometimes, if it's a small breach, you might be able to get the lender just to give you a waiver saying that it's okay and it's not a big issue. For example, maybe your financial reports were a few weeks late. Of course, that's a breach of your loan agreement. But if you go in and explain your circumstances, they may give you a waiver. But if the breach is serious, they can suspend your loan or they can even call your loan, meaning demand early payment. And that's not a situation you want to be in because called loans are very challenging. Lenders can also seize your assets and ensure that you never borrow against them again and even initiate legal action. Usually, the consequences for most breaches are far less severe and the lender will just charge additional fees or increase rates. Loan covenants are very important. It's important for you to have them listed out and that you understand exactly what you need to do to not breach any of them. You should understand what you're agreeing to, what's required of you, and the impact it can have on your business if you aren't able to abide by them. It's also worth doing this exercise so you can decide what's reasonable and potentially go back to the lender to renegotiate the covenants to some extent. Loan covenants are very important and should be taken seriously because they can seriously derail the success of your company if you don't keep on track with them. Another note, never try to hide a covenant breach from a lender. Be upfront about it. If it happens, approach the lender, describe what happened, tell them what you're doing to fix it, talk to them about what the breach is, try to get a waiver from them, but don't hide breaches from your banks. You'll find that your lenders will be much more cooperative if you're honest and upfront. Provide a reasonable explanation for what's going on and maybe even ask them for help. They're likely not going to hold one slip up against you and they're more likely to remain bullish on you because you're being transparent and honest with them. It's much better for your banking relationship for your lenders to hear and find out a problem from you not having to find them themselves. If they have to find them themselves and you break confidence, you will not get loans from banks. I promise you because they all talk to each other. Now, you can't avoid covenants, but they can be negotiated and they can be managed. That covers everything in the attracting financiers model. Next, we're going to jump into perhaps the most exciting part of the course, doing the due diligence and closing the deal.
